answers.1: Removing the tariffs and working with agriculture to re-develop trade relationships is the single most important thing to protect our agricultural economy. The rising costs for fuel and fertilizers due to the war in Iran also need to be addressed and fixed immediately. In meeting with farmers from around the district, making sure the regulatory environment is stable and transparent is very important to our export markets.
answers.2: This is a hard question. We know that regulation is at times overbearing and wasteful. Identifying those years is a real challenge. Step one, prevent industry from writing regulations. Today, industry incumbents in all sectors lobby FOR regulations that reduce competition and protect market share. This is bad. I would also like to incentivize government professionals to reduce regulatory burdens where possible. This will be a challenge, but today, the incentives are for ever MORE regulations. I would also like to change the structure of any regulatory regime so that it is based on evidence and/or some kind of cost benefit analysis.
answers.3: Yes, keeping the most vulnerable out of detention is a priority. When the Democrats take control of the House of Representatives, we regain power of the purse over the Trump Administration. We will seek to defund detention centers as soon as possible. Holding the government accountable will be a more difficult task. Today, the Republicans only seem to be ready to hold Democratic politicians and government people accountable. We will need help with the large number of reforms necessary to make sure everyone in the government is accountable and bound by the same laws.
answers.4: I stand for a fast, fair, and affordable path to legal status for all immigrants who have lived here for more than 5 years and have positively contributed to their communities. These are valued members of our community, and central Washington’s economy is stronger because of them. The Trump administration immigration enforcement is immoral and un-American. They aren’t out for the ‘worst of the worst,’ they are catching anyone they can. They are even retroactively changing immigrants’ status, and are deporting them as they try to work through the system the ‘right way.’
answers.5: Congress has ceded too much power to the executive branch. Congress should insist on oversight and public hearings to set specific, measurable and meaningful rules on what federal rule-making and decision-making are allowed in the scope of legislation. The most important thing is long term stability for the economy, businesses need time to understand the rules and regulations, and they shouldn’t change wildly because the Presidency or Congress changed partisan control. A strong economy should be a common goal.
answers.6: AI has its place, and it’s not going anywhere - there’s no doubt about that. However, data centers, which house more than just AI technology, shouldn’t place burdens on the local communities or compete for vital resources. During my 24 years in the Air Force as an IT and Cybersecurity Officer, I was tasked with understanding and overseeing these types of systems. We don’t yet know what effects these data centers or AI will have on future technology and the subsequent effects on society, but I have first hand experience with the infrastructure and hardware. And as an avid outdoorsman and father, I have a vested interest in seeing that our current actions don’t negatively impact future generations.

answers.1: Tariffs have raised consumer prices and shut down certain foreign market access for farmers and other manufacturers in the region. Ultimately, they have created a chaotic and uncertain economic environment. I don't oppose tariffs in theory, but there is a reason why it is Congress who holds the power to levy them, as the Supreme Court recently affirmed. Their imposition should be carefully considered and, ideally, paired with complementary industrial policy.
answers.2: Some of my top priorities include clean energy deployment and wildfire prevention. Often though, environmental regulations are wielded to block or delay clean energy projects and things like prescribed burns and targeted thinning. When laws meant to protect the environment end up worsening wildfire danger and carbon emissions by delaying sensible action, they aren't serving their original purpose. That doesn't mean erasing environmental regulations. As an Eagle Scout, I spent countless hours outdoors and have a deep affinity for the wild world. But there's a real balance between protecting that world and letting well-intentioned laws be abused.
answers.3: Right now, detention facility conditions are largely governed by internal ICE and CBP guidelines, rather than being plainly spelled out in federal law. The obvious flaw in this model - one the Trump administration is exploiting - is that these agencies can choose to revise or outright ignore these guidelines as they see fit. I support codifying minimum treatment standards for federal detainees into law, thereby creating a persistent legal lever for agency accountability. I also support efforts to increase Congressional access to detention facilities so that members of the legislative branch can independently verify the conditions inside.
answers.4: Well, I certainly have more work to do here. Representation starts with showing up, and I know I haven't done enough of that yet in Hispanic communities across CD4. What I can commit to: I'd be their representative the same as anyone else in this district, and work to build real relationships with the Hispanic community. Central Washington's economy runs substantially on Hispanic labor. Forging effective channels of communication between my DC and district offices with local community leaders would be an early focus of my time in Congress.
answers.5: Defending the Constitution right now starts with recognizing how much Congress has abdicated its own power to the Executive Branch, especially on the issues that matter most. Congress needs to reassert its authority and restore real balance between the branches. On party loyalty: I entered this race as an independent, later accepting the nomination of the Cascade Party of Washington, specifically because of serious critiques I have of both major parties and their complicity in DC's corruption. Throughout this campaign, I've sought input and perspective from voters across the political spectrum, and I intend to keep that ethic in Congress.
answers.6: First, we need clear regulatory guidelines on energy and water usage, and light and noise pollution, from data center facilities. I don't oppose building such facilities, but these are common sense public protections. Additionally, AI companies are betting big on this technology to become hugely profitable, but what's publicly known about training and operating costs suggests the path to profitability is harder than their public comments imply. That said, I unequivocally oppose using taxpayer money to bail out these companies if the massive private speculation in this sector turns out to have been a bad investment.

answers.1: Tariffs have raised costs on both ends. Our farmers pay more for equipment, fertilizer, and packaging, then lose export markets when other countries retaliate — and Central Washington grows far more than we can consume locally. Consumers pay higher prices for groceries and everyday goods. Tariff chaos and foreign conflicts have made it nearly impossible for farms and small businesses to plan ahead. I'll push to end unnecessary tariffs, stabilize trade relationships, and restore Congress's constitutional role in trade policy. Steady, predictable trade keeps our farms strong and prices down.
answers.2: I stand for smaller government and less waste. My first bill will eliminate federal income taxes for anyone making under $60,000 would shrink the IRS by 20-40% and save taxpayers $4-8 billion a year. I'll also stand for streamlining permitting so energy and housing projects get built instead of dying in a decade of review. We need our government to stay lean, up to date and responsive to the people. The limit: deregulation should never strip protections for worker safety, clean air and water, or fair markets. We can find the proper balance by bringing everyone to the table and finding the best solutions for our district.
answers.3:
Yes — this is personal. I'm a civil rights attorney and the son of Mexican farmworkers. Detaining children, pregnant women, and medically vulnerable people without adequate care is wrong, and due process is not optional. I will fight for common sense immigration reform. In Congress I'd push for enforceable

detention standards, independent inspections, alternatives to detention for the most vulnerable, and real oversight — Congress controls the funding and should use that power. Secure borders and humane treatment are not opposites. Government power must always be checked, especially when people's liberty and safety are at stake.
answers.4: I am second generation Mexican American and grew up as a migrant farmworker working the Yakima orchards alongside my parents and have witnessed first hand being mistreated and refused a seat at the table. Now as an attorney, I have worked hard on behalf of Hispanic clients that continue to be mistreated. As your representative, I'll make sure that our Hispanic community has a seat at the table before decisions are made, hire staff who reflect our community, and engage with everyone in our district.
answers.5: I'm running as an independent precisely because I refuse to be party-first. As an independent, I don’t answer to super PACs or political bosses in DC, I answer to you. I'll work with anyone, Republican or Democrat, when it's good for our district, and stand up to anyone when it's not. Defending the Constitution is my life's work as a civil rights attorney: due process, free speech, checks and balances. I will stay accessible to you and listen to your guidance as we navigate new challenges and opportunities for our district.
answers.6: AI is one of the most powerful and transformational technologies of our lifetimes. Just like any other technology, it can be used for good or for bad. We need the collective conscious to guide this new era, which will require all hands on deck. I'll require real transparency from data centers on energy and water use so communities can negotiate fair deals that bring jobs and tax revenue without raising everyone's power bills. I'll invest in trades and workforce training so our kids share in upside of this. Together we can make sure that this technology benefits our district.

answers.1:
Tariffs are a mixed tool with real costs. In Washington's 4th District — a major exporter of apples, cherries, wheat, hops, wine, and dairy — they have often had detrimental net effects on farmers and consumers.

On Farmers

Retaliatory Tariffs Hurt Exports: During the 2018-2019 trade tensions, China and other partners imposed counter-tariffs on U.S. agricultural products. Washington cherry and apple growers saw sharp drops in export markets, forcing sales at lower domestic prices or storage losses. Many farmers required government aid to offset losses.

Input Costs: Tariffs on steel/aluminum and other goods raised equipment, packaging, and infrastructure costs for farms and processors.

Ongoing: Lingering or new tariffs continue to create uncertainty, making long-term planning difficult for export-dependent operations.

On Consumers

Higher Prices: Tariffs function as a tax on imports, raising costs for consumer goods (electronics, vehicles, clothing, building materials). In a district already facing high energy and food costs, this adds pressure on household budgets.

Food & Energy Link: Reduced export markets can lead to domestic gluts (lowering some farm-gate prices temporarily) but overall supply chain disruptions and higher input/shipping costs contribute to elevated grocery and energy prices.

My View: Targeted, reciprocal tariffs can protect specific sectors, but broad or prolonged ones often backfire on export-heavy regions like WA-4 by inviting retaliation and raising costs for families and businesses. Better approaches include trade agreements that open markets, domestic competitiveness (e.g., energy abundance, regulatory relief), and safety-net support during disruptions. The goal should be abundant, affordable goods rather than protectionism that ultimately burdens consumers and farmers.
answers.2:
Red tape reduction (or “cutting government overreach”) is essential for growth, innovation, and affordability. I strongly favor a free-market approach with minimal government involvement, limited to core constitutional roles like enforcing contracts, preventing fraud, protecting against clear externalities (e.g., basic environmental and public safety rules), and upholding the Commerce Clause for interstate trade. Government mandates often distort markets, raise costs, and slow progress.

Priority Areas for Red Tape Cutting

Energy & Infrastructure: Streamline permitting for power plants (including nuclear and hydro upgrades), transmission lines, and pipelines. Excessive reviews delay projects and drive up energy costs for families and farms.

Agriculture & Food: Reduce overlapping federal rules on farming practices, pesticides, water use, and processing that inflate food prices. Focus on outcomes (food safety) rather than micromanaging methods.

Small Business & Housing: Cut occupational licensing, zoning overreach, and environmental reviews that block housing supply and business startups. Simplify tax code and reporting.

Healthcare: Ease restrictions on cross-state licensing, telemedicine, and price transparency to increase supply and lower costs.

Tech & Innovation: Light-touch regulation for AI, biotech, and emerging energy to accelerate breakthroughs without stifling competition.

Limits of This Approach

Free markets excel at allocating resources and spurring innovation, but basic rules are needed: clear property rights, contract enforcement, national defense, and addressing genuine market failures (e.g., certain pollution or fraud). Over-deregulation in those areas risks abuse, but the bigger problem today is excessive rules that favor incumbents and raise costs. The goal is smart, targeted government — not zero government.

This philosophy prioritizes liberty, prosperity, and evidence over mandates. It aligns with reducing burdens on Central Washington families, farmers, and businesses.
answers.3:
Yes, humane treatment of vulnerable people in immigration enforcement is a serious priority, as is the rule of law. Immigration detention exists to ensure compliance with proceedings and removal orders for those who enter or remain unlawfully. Blanket policies that ignore flight risk or danger undermine enforcement and can encourage more illegal crossings. At the same time, conditions must meet legal standards, and the most vulnerable—pregnant/postpartum women, young children, and those with serious medical needs—should generally be handled through alternatives to detention where feasible. Current Context (Evidence-Based) ICE has formal directives generally directing against detaining pregnant, postpartum, or nursing individuals except in limited cases (e.g., serious flight risk, national security, or criminal history). Official standards (National Detention Standards) require medical care, nutrition, and restrictions on restraints for pregnant women. However, oversight reports, congressional visits, and investigations have documented deficiencies in some facilities, including delays in medical care, nutrition issues, and overcrowding—particularly amid surges in enforcement. Advocacy reports highlight specific cases of miscarriages, inadequate prenatal care, and other problems. Official data shows dozens of pregnant women in custody at points in recent years, with some miscarriages reported. DHS OIG and other oversight bodies conduct inspections; compliance varies by facility. These issues are not new across administrations but require ongoing fixes. Priorities and Realistic Steps Yes, keeping the most vulnerable out of detention (where safe) and improving conditions would be priorities: Expand and Improve Alternatives to Detention (ATD): Prioritize community-based programs with case management, check-ins, and technology (e.g., ICE’s ISAP) for low-risk pregnant women, families, and medically vulnerable individuals. Past programs showed high compliance rates (often 90%+ for court appearances). Steps: Increase funding and capacity for ATD; direct ICE to default to release/monitoring for qualifying vulnerable cases unless specific risks justify detention; partner with NGOs for support services.

Strengthen Medical Care and Conditions: Mandate compliance with standards for prenatal/postnatal care, nutrition, mental health, and privacy. Require prompt transfers to appropriate medical facilities. Steps: Expand independent medical oversight (e.g., via DHS Office for Civil Rights and Civil Liberties or external reviewers); improve electronic health records and continuity of care; increase staffing and audits at facilities. Targeted Protections: Formalize stronger presumptions against detaining pregnant/postpartum individuals and unaccompanied/minor children except in truly exceptional circumstances. Ensure family unity where possible through alternatives. Holding Government Accountable Abuse of power or willful neglect is unacceptable and should be investigated and prosecuted where evidence supports criminal violations (e.g., deliberate denial of necessary care leading to harm). This requires facts, not just allegations. Transparency and Oversight: Demand timely, detailed public reporting on deaths, serious incidents, and medical care in custody. Support robust DHS OIG inspections (unannounced) and congressional oversight visits. Whistleblowers and Investigations: Protect and incentivize reporting of violations; pursue independent reviews of patterns in complaints. Prosecution and Reform: Where credible evidence of abuse exists, hold individuals and contractors accountable through DOJ or other mechanisms. Use data from OIG and facility audits to drive systemic fixes (e.g., closing or reforming persistently deficient facilities). Root Cause Focus: Secure borders and efficient legal processing reduce the need for prolonged detention and associated strains. A free society enforces its laws humanely. Effective enforcement + strong oversight + expanded alternatives for the vulnerable is the realistic path. Blanket release policies or weakened enforcement create different problems (backlogs, recidivism, public safety risks). Reforms should be evidence-driven, focused on compliance with existing standards, and aimed at genuine accountability rather than political narratives. If specific reports or facilities in/near the district are of concern, more targeted oversight or legislation can address them. Rule of law means both enforcing borders and ensuring those in custody are treated according to standards. Serious allegations deserve serious investigation.
answers.4: I reject the premise of this question. Framing any group as "underrepresented" by ethnicity or using hyphenated identities divides Americans and implies that elected officials should prioritize one group over others. If elected, I will represent every resident of the 4th District equally— farmers, workers, families, businesses—without regard to race, ethnicity, or background. All are Americans first. The Hispanic community in Central Washington is a vital part of our agricultural and economic backbone. Many are here legally and contribute tremendously; their success is American success. Policies that strengthen the economy—secure water rights, lower energy and input costs, reduced regulations on farming, job opportunities, and strong rule of law—benefit them and everyone else. I will fight for those outcomes because they work for the entire district. Legal immigrants who followed the rules deserve full respect and opportunity. For those here illegally, I will uphold federal immigration law while ensuring due process and humane treatment under the Constitution. I will not support identity-based quotas, preferences, or special carve-outs. Decisions will be based on what is best for the district’s prosperity, safety, and shared values—not on dividing people into groups. Unity as Americans, equal application of the law, and opportunity through hard work is the only sustainable path forward.
answers.5:
I will defend the U.S. Constitution vigorously as the supreme law of the land. Having been born and raised in a socialist/communist system, I understand firsthand how distortions of law, erosion of rights, and centralized power destroy freedom and prosperity. I will fight against any misapplication or expansion of government that undermines individual liberties, due process, free speech, property rights, or the separation of powers.

Bipartisanship: I am not a “party first, party always” candidate. I will work with anyone— Democrat, Republican, or Independent—when it serves the country’s and district’s best interests. Good policy is good policy, regardless of origin. I will cross party lines on issues like infrastructure, water rights, energy abundance, and fiscal responsibility if they deliver results for Central Washington families and farmers.

Representing All Constituents: My job is to represent every person in the 4th District—urban, rural, agricultural workers, business owners, legal immigrants, longtime residents—without favoring any ideology, ethnicity, or party faction. I will hold regular town halls, listen to diverse voices (including those who disagree), and base decisions on evidence, the Constitution, and what strengthens our communities, economy, and security. No one gets special treatment; everyone gets equal protection under the law.

I answer to the Constitution and the people of this district—not to any party machine. My pledge is simple: America first, district first, principles over partisanship.
answers.6:
As an engineer myself, I share your excitement about AI’s potential to accelerate scientific research, improve information processing, and drive innovation that benefits everyone. At the same time, I agree we must approach it cautiously and judiciously. AI should empower people, not replace critical thinking—students relying on it for homework short-circuit their own learning. Responsible growth means protecting the public from bearing unfair costs or risks.

Key Priorities:

Energy Responsibility: AI data centers must not divert power from homes and businesses. They should finance or directly develop their own dedicated energy sources—especially advanced nuclear power—for reliable, carbon-free electricity. This prevents shortages and keeps energy abundant and affordable for all.

Land Protection: Prohibit building AI facilities on prime agricultural land. Prioritize brownfields, industrial zones, or areas with existing infrastructure.

Education & Guardrails: Promote digital literacy so the public understands both benefits and risks. Support light-touch rules focused on transparency, safety standards, and preventing misuse, while avoiding heavy mandates that stifle innovation.

No Public Bailouts: Ensure the industry bears its own infrastructure and environmental costs through market mechanisms and user-pays models.

This approach encourages rapid progress while safeguarding families, farmers, and the grid. AI can be a powerful tool for American prosperity when developed responsibly.

answers.1: Tariffs are a tool to protect American workers and farmers from unfair trade, especially China's predatory practices. In WA-04, they've helped shield our agriculture from dumped imports, supporting family farms and ranchers like mine in Prosser. Short-term costs exist and I do not want to diminish those as they are plentiful for our area, but long-term tariffs encourage domestic manufacturing and fairer deals. As an engineer, I focus on results: renegotiating trade agreements that put our district's producers first, lowering overall costs through competition and reduced reliance on foreign supply chains while increasing export opportunities.
answers.2: As a manufacturing engineer and small business founder, I strongly support smart deregulation to cut wasteful red tape that burdens farmers, ranchers, and all of us here in Central Washington. Priorities include excessive environmental rules harming irrigation and agriculture, overreaching federal agencies especially with land and water use, and bureaucratic hurdles for energy and small businesses. Limits matter: we must protect public safety, private property rights, and the environment without ideological overreach. True abundance comes from freeing innovation and local decision-making, not unchecked government.
answers.3:
If we are not humane then we are not human not to mention American! The bigger picture is however interesting… Securing our border is a humanitarian and national security mandate. I support humane conditions for all detainees and prioritizing vulnerable individuals—pregnant women, children, and the medically fragile—for expedited processing or alternatives to detention when appropriate. Realistic steps include increased oversight, partnerships with faith-based and local groups for care, and technology for efficient, transparent operations. Accountability demands strict adherence to law, ending catch-and-release, and

reforming a broken system that endangers everyone, including our American communities.
answers.4: Our Hispanic neighbors are vital to Central Washington agriculture, economy, and communities. Many have been here for decades and fall into a wildly different category then many that came for freebies the last few years. As a job creator who employed many veterans and diverse workers, I'll focus on policies that grow our shared prosperity: strong border security to protect wages, excellent education and vocational training, reduced regulations on farms and small businesses, and economic opportunity for all families. Decisions will be based on merit, rule of law, and what strengthens rural Central Washington for everyone.
answers.5: I will defend the Constitution as written with limited government, individual rights, free speech, 2nd Amendment, and secure elections. Yes, I'll work on any good ideas that benefit us, as I always have as an engineer solving real problems. I'm not a "party first" candidate. Everyone can see that I’ve been attacked universally regardless of party. I don’t believe we need to have perfectly aligned values and principles to be able to live cohesively but we must have mutual respect and decorum; I'm a problem-solver accountable to all constituents. I'll hold regular town halls open to any rational discussion, use my eWA Helpline, and listen across ideologies to represent rural values, farmers, veterans, and working families effectively.
answers.6: I’ve already drafted a document that initiates a worldwide board to ensure human life is held preeminent within all AI. Look at EthicalAIAct.com. As an engineer, I embrace AI's potential to boost productivity in agriculture, manufacturing, healthcare, and other areas. Responsible growth, however, requires that our structure of liberty is secure along with intellectual property, data privacy, and American workers. We must also reject heavy-handed regulation that stifles innovation. I'll prioritize public-private partnerships, workforce training, and safeguards so taxpayers aren't left "holding the bag." Progress with accountability ensures AI serves families and strengthens our economy, not replaces it.

answers.1: Tariffs severely raise the cost of doing business. Tariffs are not good for the market and should only be used in precise narrow circumstances if and only if it is absolutely necessary to make a point or to help open a market or to police a market. In general tariffs are bad economic policy. It is a way of going to war with a nation; it makes no sense if you are trying to do business with a nation; it is the opposite direction, war.
answers.2:
Start up and small businesses need sweeping deregulation to have a chance to survive. Family farms and ranches also need reregulation. All three classes/groups should be tax exempt. Some 98% of start up business fail in the first 10 years and the farm and ranch business parallel church attendance over the past 100 years, same decline. All wasteful and absurd regulation that costs the taxpayer and achieves nothing of value should be eliminated. Some activities must be regulated for safety and to protect the environment.

The key question is what is or was the reason for the regulation? Does it still serve the purpose intended and would the nation/industry be better off without it? In general this nation has far far far over-regulated itself. A substantial amount of the rules and regulations on the books need to be repealed.
answers.3: ICE and DHS ring of unconstitutionality. ICE should be set on a limited time to termination, by January 2027 it should be terminated, disbanded. Clearly we have too many federal agencies and largely they do not do the job they are established to do. There should be measures put in place that constantly evaluate whether a federal job is actually necessary and if anything or enough to merit payment for the post is actually accomplished, if not the job needs elimination. Our federal government is so massive and so wasteful there is no feasible way to account or track progress or spending. This has to be stopped if the agency cannot be managed, monitored and all funds accounted for then it has to be eliminated. Perhaps coordinate with some other agency like the Red Cross or U.N. services to provide separately and more adequately for those with special needs. Perhaps Catholic Charities or the United Way, by being creative something could be done to better the care.
answers.4: I have worked for Medicare/Medicaid, Community Health Plan of Washington for 6 years. I am trained as an M.D. by the UAG International School of Medicine in Guadalajara, Mexico. And I have studied Spanish in Spain and in Mexico. No other candidate is as keen on this issue as I am and I would do everything in my power to see that the Hispanics are equally represented by my bilingual Office in Congress. I have also worked side by side with them in the orchards and in plowing the fields of the 4th Congressional District. They have my ear, literally and I will not tolerate them being treated unfairly and provide every opportunity possible for their progress and prosperity. I am their candidate for sure. I will not allow them to be slighted in any legislation I vote in favor of passing into law.
answers.5: From January to this day I have publicly preached that we must cooperate and coordinate to get anything done in Washington. I will shake hands and make all the friends I can. I will work with anyone to help keep this nation free. I am a member of the U.S. Supreme Court Bar and the USPTO Bar. I will use all my education, knowledge and abilities to prosper the 4th District and to defend our constitution against becoming “The Technological Republic” of Alex Karp and his companions. I will be the new Will Rogers and the best statesman I can by putting our citizens above my personal desires and ego. I will gladly hold town hall meetings and openly meet with any and everyone in this district from the Columbia river to the Canadian border. The democrats will take back the House. Any representative that will not work with the democrats will accomplish absolutely nothing and will only take the wage and the seat from someone else who could work with the democrats. I have an agricultural background, a legal background, a patent law background, a chemical engineering background and a medical background as well as international travel and study plus 5 degrees and 50 years of preparation for this office. No other candidate can offer the wealth of knowledge and ideas I can bring to the table to solve our problems and accomplish results for the people of the 4th district of Washington.
answers.6:
A.I. must be regulated and carefully monitored and made transparent. It must not be allowed to rule or ruin the lives of citizens. It must not be allowed to write any legal documents of any kind whatsoever. And it must be strictly prohibited from use to bypass our constitutional rights and civil liberties. A.I. is more dangerous than atomic power. And it must be severely restricted in its use and restricted from growing beyond its stated limited use. A.I. should not be allowed to be built west of the Mississippi river except in East Texas and areas with abundant water. There is not adequate water in the west and it must be banned in all western states. There is no water for it.

The Palantir Corporation should be investigated for constitutional violations of citizens and that company should only be allowed to be used in offshore operations. Any A.I. company should not be allowed access to any information from the IRS, Medicaid, Medicare or any domestic agency. Its role in military operations should be reviewed and regulated and under strict scrutiny. A.I. should never be allowed to bypass human judgment and control. Given the leadership of Palantir Inc. it may be wise just to ban this company from operating within the U.S.A. No one knows what the future with A.I. will be. Its potential to be misused and to harm humanity is vast and unappreciated. Failure to limit A.I. could well be the end of all that is good and precious in this nation. Inadequate control, rule and regulation of A.I. is a neglect of our responsibility to each other and especially to all future generations who will inherit what we do, good or bad.

answers.1:
Tariffs have raised costs for consumers and created uncertainty for farmers in Washington’s 4th District. For families, tariffs often act like a tax on imports, increasing prices on food, equipment, building materials, vehicles, and everyday products.

For farmers, the effect can be serious. Central Washington depends on export markets for apples, cherries, wheat, wine, and other products. When countries retaliate, farmers can lose market access, face lower prices, and pay more for inputs.

I support targeted trade enforcement, but broad tariffs should be used carefully because they can hurt the families and farmers they are meant to help.
answers.2:
I support regulatory reform when it lowers costs, speeds up construction, and makes it easier to build and produce in America again. Too often, federal rules, permitting delays, duplicate programs, and unnecessary paperwork raise the cost of housing, energy, infrastructure, farming, and small business operations.

I would focus on faster permitting for energy, transmission, roads, bridges, housing infrastructure, water storage, broadband, and domestic manufacturing. I also support reviewing outdated federal programs.

But deregulation has limits. Government still has a responsibility to protect clean water, public safety, consumers, workers, and the environment.
answers.3:
I support strong immigration enforcement, but it must be lawful, orderly, and accountable. Illegal immigration places real costs on citizens, taxpayers, schools, hospitals, housing, and communities. The federal government must secure the border and remove people here illegally, especially those who commit crimes or threaten public safety.

At the same time, detention facilities must meet basic standards. Children, pregnant and postpartum women, and medically vulnerable detainees should receive medical care, nutrition, privacy, and due process.

I support detention standards, inspections, body cameras, warrants for private residences, consequences for failed care, and legal sponsorship for spouses of American citizens.
answers.4:
Central Washington’s Hispanic community is essential to our agriculture, small businesses, workforce, schools, churches, and local economy. Their voices should not only be heard during campaign season; they should be part of decision-making year-round.

I would meet directly with Hispanic community leaders, farmworkers, growers, small business owners, parents, students, churches, and local organizations before supporting policies that affect them. Congressional offices should be accessible and respectful, including through bilingual outreach where needed.

My focus would be affordability, education, workforce training, safe communities, small business growth, water and agriculture infrastructure, and making sure Hispanic concerns are represented in Congress.
answers.5:
I will defend the Constitution by protecting rights of all Americans, including free speech, religious liberty, the Second Amendment, privacy, due process, the Fourth Amendment, and limits on government power.

I am a Republican, but not a “party first, party always” candidate. I will cross party lines when it is good for the district and the country. I will not rubber-stamp spending bills, open-ended foreign conflicts, warrantless FISA surveillance, unconstitutional executive actions, or broad tariffs.

I would listen to farmers, workers, small businesses, veterans, seniors, parents, students, law enforcement, and people across every community, including those who disagree with me.
answers.6:
As an engineer myself, I believe America should lead in AI, not fall behind. AI can improve agriculture, healthcare, education, cybersecurity, manufacturing, government services, and small business productivity.

But responsible growth means the public should not be left holding the bag. Large AI and data center projects should not raise local energy costs, strain water resources, or damage the environment without accountability. I support water protections, transparency around energy and water usage, utility offsets if local costs rise, and requiring major projects to help pay for infrastructure impacts.

When taxpayers directly fund breakthroughs, taxpayers deserve a fair return.
